learning.summary: A file system defines how an operating system organizes, stores, and retrieves data on a formatted partition. Different operating systems support different file systems, and the selected file system determines how data is managed.
learning.explanation:
A partition cannot reliably store files until it has been formatted with a file system. During formatting, the operating system creates the structures needed to organize files, directories, and metadata.

The file system acts as the interface between the operating system and storage media. It determines how files are named, located, protected, and managed. Different operating systems commonly use different default file systems, although some are designed for cross-platform compatibility.

facts.text: A partition must be formatted with a file system before it can store data.
A file system provides the structure an operating system uses to read and write data.
Different operating systems support different file systems.

examples.text: Formatting a new partition as NTFS allows Windows to organize and manage files on that partition.

examTips: Know that formatting applies a file system to a partition.
Understand that operating systems use different default file systems.
commonMistakes: Confusing partition creation with partition formatting.
Assuming every operating system uses the same file system.
scenarios: Choose an appropriate file system when preparing a new storage device.
pbqIdeas: Match operating systems to appropriate file systems after formatting storage.

reviewNotes: Explanation includes general file-system behavior beyond the quoted transcript but is standard IT knowledge.
